title: CVM (Controller VM) Health
exclusions: Not covered here: the template-style sourcetype string `nutanix:cvm` is not something `Splunk_TA_nutanix` from Splunkbase 5076 publishes; the real per-service feed is `nutanix:cluster:services_status`, identity comes from `nutanix:host` with `cvm_ip` (sometimes labeled like `controller_vm_backplane_ip` in UIs) or `vm_type` filters when your estate mixes roles. The cluster-aggregate 0–100 health score and cluster-wide `down_svcs` roll-up in UC-19.2.25 uses `nutanix:cluster` plus the same service stream at cluster scope, which is a different question than a single CVM’s tuple. CVM CPU, RAM, and Stargate average latency as a capacity story stay in UC-19.2.14. The Prism Central management API plane, UI reachability, and PC-level alarms belong with UC-19.2.12 and the PC alert family, not with per-node CVM data-plane rows. CVM free-space and reserve thresholds are a capacity axis, not the Genesis service map. Third-party only-NCC HTTP shims you bolt in beside 5076 are your parser problem until they emit the same fields.
evidence: A saved search (for example `ss_uc_19_2_7_cvm_data_plane_liveness_v1`) scheduled every five minutes, with a PDF or CSV of the `table` output archived to a restricted `idx_evidence` on each Sev-1, proves Splunk was polling per-CVM `service_name` and `service_status` from 5076 sourcetypes, not a fictional `nutanix:cvm` feed, and that owner routing from `nutanix_cluster_owner` was live at the time of review.
dataSources: `(index=hci OR index=nutanix)` (match where `Splunk_TA_nutanix` writes) with sourcetype `nutanix:cluster:services_status`: one row per `(cluster_name, cvm_ip, service_name)` with `service_status` in {`UP`, `DOWN`, `NA`, `kStarting`, `kStopping`, `kForwarding`} plus optional `pid`, `start_time`, `version`, and alias fields `cvmIp`, `host_ip`, `serviceName`, `serviceStatus` on some TA builds. Enrich with `nutanix:host` for `host_uuid` / `hostUuid`, `node_uuid` / `nodeUuid`, `name`, `cvm_ip` / `service_vmaddress`, `host_in_maintenance_mode` / `hostInMaintenanceMode`, `node_position`, `block_serial`, `state`, `vm_type` (filter `Controller VM` if your host stream mixes node types). There is no vendor `nutanix:cvm` sourcetype from Splunkbase 5076; identity comes from the host and services_status streams only.
app: Install the add-on folder `Splunk_TA_nutanix` ([Splunkbase 5076](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/5076)) on a data-collection tier that can reach Prism Element or Prism Central HTTPS 9440 and on Search Heads. Pair with a thin wrapper app that ships `lookups/nutanix_cluster_owner.csv` and `default/transforms.conf` for `nutanix_cluster_owner` (same `cluster` key shape as UC-19.2.25) without editing vendor `default/`.
spl:
`comment("UC-19.2.7 — CVM (Controller VM) per-node data-plane health: Stargate/Cassandra/Genesis gating, Cassandra kForwarding treated as healthy, host maintenance suppression, coalesce of camelCase and snake_case, nutanix_cluster_owner lookup. Replace index= if your deployment differs.")`
(index=hci OR index=nutanix) sourcetype="nutanix:cluster:services_status" earliest=-2h@h
| eval cluster_name=coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, name)
| eval cvm_ip=coalesce(cvm_ip, cvmIp, host_ip, hostIp)
| eval service_name=coalesce(service_name, serviceName)
| eval service_status=coalesce(service_status, serviceStatus)
| eval skey=lower(trim(tostring(service_name)))
| where isnotnull(cvm_ip) AND len(trim(tostring(cvm_ip)))>0
| where skey IN ("stargate", "cassandra", "genesis", "curator", "acropolis", "pithos", "hera")
| eval st=trim(tostring(service_status))
| eval u=upper(st)
| eval l=lower(st)
| stats latest(st) as st latest(_time) as last_status_ev BY cluster_name, cvm_ip, skey
| eval u=upper(st)
| eval l=lower(st)
| eval bad_stg=if(skey="stargate" AND u!="UP", 1, 0)
| eval bad_cas=if(skey="cassandra" AND (u!="UP" AND l!="kforwarding"), 1, 0)
| eval bad_gen=if(skey="genesis" AND u!="UP", 1, 0)
| eval bad_cur=if(skey="curator" AND u="DOWN", 1, 0)
| eval bad_acp=if(skey="acropolis" AND (u!="UP" AND u!="NA"), 1, 0)
| eval bad_pit=if(skey="pithos" AND u="DOWN", 1, 0)
| eval bad_her=if(skey="hera" AND u="DOWN", 1, 0)
| stats max(bad_stg) as bad_stg max(bad_cas) as bad_cas max(bad_gen) as bad_gen max(bad_cur) as bad_cur max(bad_acp) as bad_acp max(bad_pit) as bad_pit max(bad_her) as bad_her max(last_status_ev) as last_status_ev BY cluster_name, cvm_ip
| eval has_core=if((bad_stg=1) OR (bad_cas=1) OR (bad_gen=1), 1, 0)
| eval cvm_severity=case(has_core=1, "critical", (bad_cur=1) OR (bad_acp=1), "high", (bad_pit=1) OR (bad_her=1), "medium", 1=1, null())
| where isnotnull(cvm_severity)
| join type=left max=0 cluster_name, cvm_ip [
  search (index=hci OR index=nutanix) sourcetype="nutanix:host" earliest=-2h@h
  | eval cluster_name=coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, name)
  | eval cvm_ip=coalesce(cvm_ip, cvmIp, service_vmaddress, host_ip, hostIp)
  | eval m_raw=coalesce(host_in_maintenance_mode, hostInMaintenanceMode, "false")
  | eval in_maint=if(match(lower(tostring(m_raw)), "(true|1|t|yes)"), 1, 0)
  | eval node_uuid=coalesce(node_uuid, nodeUuid)
  | eval host_uuid=coalesce(host_uuid, hostUuid)
  | stats latest(in_maint) as in_maint, latest(name) as host_name, latest(node_position) as node_position, latest(node_uuid) as node_uuid, latest(host_uuid) as host_uuid, latest(block_serial) as block_serial, latest(_time) as last_host_ev BY cluster_name, cvm_ip
]
| where (in_maint=0) OR isnull(in_maint)
| eval cluster=cluster_name
| lookup local=true nutanix_cluster_owner cluster OUTPUT business_service, owner_email, change_freeze_until
| eval sevord=case(cvm_severity="critical", 3, cvm_severity="high", 2, 1, 1)
| table cluster_name, cvm_ip, cvm_severity, bad_stg, bad_cas, bad_gen, bad_cur, bad_acp, bad_pit, bad_her, host_name, node_position, node_uuid, host_uuid, block_serial, last_status_ev, last_host_ev, business_service, owner_email, change_freeze_until
| sort 0 - sevord, cluster_name, cvm_ip
description: Observes each Nutanix Controller VM as a first-class object keyed by `(cluster_name, cvm_ip)` from `nutanix:cluster:services_status`, with `stats latest(_time) BY cluster_name, cvm_ip, service_name` to collapse each modular-input poll without double-counting the same service row. The search treats the Genesis-supervised Stargate, Cassandra, and Genesis processes as the hard gate for data-plane liveness, treats `kForwarding` on Cassandra as quorum-safe, joins `nutanix:host` for rack-friendly identity (`node_position`, `block_serial`, `node_uuid`, `host_uuid`) and to drop hosts flagged in maintenance, and attaches governance columns from the `nutanix_cluster_owner` lookup so each failing CVM row is actionable. Rows represent only CVMs that still violate a policy after those filters, not a cluster-rolled health score and not a capacity signal.
value: When a Controller VM on one node is missing a storage or metadata process, the cluster keeps guest workloads up by redirecting I/O and metadata work to peers, so users feel latency and the failure domain grows even though VMs still power on. Surfacing a sorted queue of unhealthy CVMs with owner, serial, and backplane address shrinks the time the storage team needs to find the one node that matters during an incident, gives change managers a fair chance to see overlap with a maintenance ticket before paging, and protects weekend batch windows by not treating every blip as a hypervisor host fault when the CVM is the real choke point.
implementation: Run `Splunk_TA_nutanix` against Prism Element (or a Prism Central path the TA supports) so `nutanix:cluster:services_status` and `nutanix:host` land in a shared index. Place `nutanix_cluster_owner.csv` beside a `transforms.conf` stanza that keys on the same `cluster` string you see in Prism. Schedule the `spl` every five minutes, throttle on `cluster_name`+`cvm_ip`, and route `critical` to the Nutanix platform queue; keep `change_freeze_until` in the lookup for governance-only annotation rather than a silent drop unless your CAB explicitly wants freeze-aware suppression for non-core classes.
visualization: (1) Sortable `table` of `cluster_name`, `cvm_ip`, `cvm_severity`, per-service fault bits, `host_name`, `node_position`, `block_serial`, `owner_email`. (2) Single value: count of CVMs with `cvm_severity=critical` in the last lookback. (3) Drilled detail panel: raw `nutanix:host` for one `cvm_ip` in the same window. (4) Optional matrix panel: child search with `xyseries` of `service_name` × per-CVM for war-room, not the alert body.

references.title: Nutanix Developer — REST and Prism context

knownFalsePositives: During a rolling AOS upgrade, Genesis briefly stops child processes on each node in order; you can see Stargate or a companion drop for thirty to ninety seconds in `nutanix:cluster:services_status` with a matching change ticket, which is not the same as surprise day-two downtime. Cassandra often sits in a `kForwarding` ring state through a ring rebalance; that state is part of a safe metadata move, and you must not treat it like a `DOWN` line item once your SPL treats kForwarding as healthy. Genesis may read `kStarting` for a minute after a CVM reboot or IP stack bounce; hold the page unless it stays out of `UP` over several polls. Curator is intentionally quiet during some disk scans; a throttled or sleeping Curator is different from `DOWN` in Genesis meaning—compare timestamps to maintenance. When operations move a CVM to a new backplane address during switch work, the same physical node can appear as a new `cvm_ip` until the next full host poll, which looks like a fresh gap but tracks to one maintenance window, not a second outage. Finally, a synthetic harness that replays two copies of the same JSON line can make `latest()` look noisy; guard lab indexes with a tag.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites

• `Splunk_TA_nutanix` (Splunkbase 5076) on a collection node with outbound TCP 9440 to Prism, plus the same add-on on Search Heads. Pin the TA in change control; field renames in minor versions are why every `coalesce()` list below exists.
• Index: examples use `(index=hci OR index=nutanix)`; your `inputs.conf` index must be allowed on the `nutanix_ops` (or similar) role.
• `lookups/nutanix_cluster_owner.csv` with at least `cluster` (string that matches the Prism `cluster_name` you expect in `nutanix:cluster:services_status`), `business_service`, `owner_email`, and `change_freeze_until` in ISO-8601 form. The lookup key is the string form of the cluster, not a UUID, unless you intentionally standardized on UUID in the CSV—if you do, add a `cluster_uuid` `eval` join in dev first.
• `default/transforms.conf` in a wrapper app:

```ini
[nutanix_cluster_owner]
filename = nutanix_cluster_owner.csv
case_sensitive_match = true
min_matches = 0
```

export the transform to every role that runs the alert.

### **Step 1 — Validate the data feed**

Field truth for `nutanix:cluster:services_status` (per TA build, verify with `| fieldsummary` in dev after each upgrade): `cluster_name` (or mixed-case alias), `cvm_ip` (sometimes `host_ip`), `service_name`, `service_status`, optional `pid`, `start_time`, `version`. Status strings come from Genesis: `UP`, `DOWN`, `NA`, `kStarting`, `kStopping`, and Cassandra-only `kForwarding`. `NA` is often expected for Acropolis on ESXi, so the SPL in Step 2 allows `NA` for Acropolis while still flagging a true `DOWN` on AHV clusters where Acropolis is in play.

`nutanix:host` is the CVM identity row: `cvm_ip` (aka backplane in many UIs), `name`, `node_position`, `block_serial`, `node_uuid` / `nodeUuid`, `host_uuid` / `hostUuid`, and `host_in_maintenance_mode` (boolean-like). A sample `nutanix:cluster:services_status` event after JSON extraction looks like: `_time=2026-04-26T01:30:00Z`, `cluster_name=hci-prod-eu`, `cvm_ip=10.4.12.21`, `service_name=Stargate`, `service_status=UP`, `pid=3145`, `version=6.8.1`. A second line for the same CVM at the same poll might show `service_name=Cassandra` with `service_status=kForwarding`, which the alert treats as healthy for Cassandra only.

Index proof: `| tstats count where (index=hci OR index=nutanix) sourcetype="nutanix:cluster:services_status" by sourcetype` must be non-zero. If only `nutanix:host` works, the modular input that drives services may be disabled or pointed at a PC endpoint that does not emit that stream—read `$SPLUNK_HOME/var/log/splunk/splunk_ta_nutanix*.log` (exact filename varies by build) before you change SPL.

### **Step 2 — Run the SPL**

Paste the `spl` field from this use case into Search, set the time picker to a two-hour window that overlaps a known maintenance if you are doing a safe dry run, then save as a Report to prove parity before you schedule an Alert.

The first line is the `comment` macro so scheduled-search audit logs and peer reviews show a human label even if the saved-search description in Splunk is blank. If your deployment truly lacks a `comment` macro, add a one-line `macros.conf` that aliases `comment(1)` to a harmless `noop`, then restore the repository `spl` text—do not fork two different canon strings long term.

Fenced for lab notebooks (must match the top-level `spl` field in the file after you resolve macros):

```spl
`comment("UC-19.2.7 — CVM (Controller VM) per-node data-plane health: Stargate/Cassandra/Genesis gating, Cassandra kForwarding treated as healthy, host maintenance suppression, coalesce of camelCase and snake_case, nutanix_cluster_owner lookup. Replace index= if your deployment differs.")`
(index=hci OR index=nutanix) sourcetype="nutanix:cluster:services_status" earliest=-2h@h
| eval cluster_name=coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, name)
| eval cvm_ip=coalesce(cvm_ip, cvmIp, host_ip, hostIp)
| eval service_name=coalesce(service_name, serviceName)
| eval service_status=coalesce(service_status, serviceStatus)
| eval skey=lower(trim(tostring(service_name)))
| where isnotnull(cvm_ip) AND len(trim(tostring(cvm_ip)))>0
| where skey IN ("stargate", "cassandra", "genesis", "curator", "acropolis", "pithos", "hera")
| eval st=trim(tostring(service_status))
| eval u=upper(st)
| eval l=lower(st)
| stats latest(st) as st latest(_time) as last_status_ev BY cluster_name, cvm_ip, skey
| eval u=upper(st)
| eval l=lower(st)
| eval bad_stg=if(skey="stargate" AND u!="UP", 1, 0)
| eval bad_cas=if(skey="cassandra" AND (u!="UP" AND l!="kforwarding"), 1, 0)
| eval bad_gen=if(skey="genesis" AND u!="UP", 1, 0)
| eval bad_cur=if(skey="curator" AND u="DOWN", 1, 0)
| eval bad_acp=if(skey="acropolis" AND (u!="UP" AND u!="NA"), 1, 0)
| eval bad_pit=if(skey="pithos" AND u="DOWN", 1, 0)
| eval bad_her=if(skey="hera" AND u="DOWN", 1, 0)
| stats max(bad_stg) as bad_stg max(bad_cas) as bad_cas max(bad_gen) as bad_gen max(bad_cur) as bad_cur max(bad_acp) as bad_acp max(bad_pit) as bad_pit max(bad_her) as bad_her max(last_status_ev) as last_status_ev BY cluster_name, cvm_ip
| eval has_core=if((bad_stg=1) OR (bad_cas=1) OR (bad_gen=1), 1, 0)
| eval cvm_severity=case(has_core=1, "critical", (bad_cur=1) OR (bad_acp=1), "high", (bad_pit=1) OR (bad_her=1), "medium", 1=1, null())
| where isnotnull(cvm_severity)
| join type=left max=0 cluster_name, cvm_ip [
  search (index=hci OR index=nutanix) sourcetype="nutanix:host" earliest=-2h@h
  | eval cluster_name=coalesce(cluster_name, clusterName, name)
  | eval cvm_ip=coalesce(cvm_ip, cvmIp, service_vmaddress, host_ip, hostIp)
  | eval m_raw=coalesce(host_in_maintenance_mode, hostInMaintenanceMode, "false")
  | eval in_maint=if(match(lower(tostring(m_raw)), "(true|1|t|yes)"), 1, 0)
  | eval node_uuid=coalesce(node_uuid, nodeUuid)
  | eval host_uuid=coalesce(host_uuid, hostUuid)
  | stats latest(in_maint) as in_maint, latest(name) as host_name, latest(node_position) as node_position, latest(node_uuid) as node_uuid, latest(host_uuid) as host_uuid, latest(block_serial) as block_serial, latest(_time) as last_host_ev BY cluster_name, cvm_ip
]
| where (in_maint=0) OR isnull(in_maint)
| eval cluster=cluster_name
| lookup local=true nutanix_cluster_owner cluster OUTPUT business_service, owner_email, change_freeze_until
| eval sevord=case(cvm_severity="critical", 3, cvm_severity="high", 2, 1, 1)
| table cluster_name, cvm_ip, cvm_severity, bad_stg, bad_cas, bad_gen, bad_cur, bad_acp, bad_pit, bad_her, host_name, node_position, node_uuid, host_uuid, block_serial, last_status_ev, last_host_ev, business_service, owner_email, change_freeze_until
| sort 0 - sevord, cluster_name, cvm_ip
```

Why `stats` twice: the first `stats` collapses each poll to `latest` per triple `(cluster_name, cvm_ip, service key)`; the second `stats` flips the long service list to one CVM row with boolean bit fields so severity math stays readable in an alert. The `join` is intentionally `max=0` on matching keys; if a CVM is missing in `nutanix:host`, you still see the service fault but `host_name` is null, which nudges you to open a TA collection gap ticket rather than assuming the node is fine.

### **Step 3 — Validate**

(1) Pick a lab node in Prism Element: note `cvm_ip` and a deliberate service line (for example Stargate). Run a raw search: `(index=hci OR index=nutanix) sourcetype="nutanix:cluster:services_status" cvm_ip=<ip> service_name=Stargate earliest=-1h` and confirm the newest `last_status_ev` is within the TA poll period. You are checking per-IP service rows, not a cluster-aggregate score, so a healthy cluster can still return rows for this CVM; that is a different pass than the UC-19.2.25 proof where you compared `cluster_health_score` to the /cluster view.

(2) Open the CVM or Services view in Prism and compare the same three strings: `Stargate`, `Cassandra`, `Genesis`. If Prism shows a green that disagrees with Splunk, the usual cause is a field alias change—run `| fieldsummary` and extend `coalesce` before you file a TAC case.

(3) Maintenance suppression: set a test host in Planned Maintenance in Prism, confirm `host_in_maintenance_mode` is true in `nutanix:host`, rerun the full UC search, and confirm the CVM disappears. Undo maintenance after the test.

(4) Lookup sanity: `| inputlookup nutanix_cluster_owner | head 5` and compare the `cluster` column to a live `stats values(cluster_name)` from the index; a single trailing space in the CSV pages the wrong person.

(5) Cassandra: inject or capture a `kForwarding` event in dev and show that `bad_cas` remains zero, while a forced `kStarting` in a short reboot window either clears within two minutes or re-fires the alert—tune the schedule, not the meaning of the state.

(6) Performance: a five-minute window over thousands of CVMs should still finish on a `parallel` search head; if the join dominates, pre-build a five-minute summary with `outputlookup` on `cluster_name,cvm_ip` in a `summary_index` job—only if capacity requires it, because you lose second-level freshness.

(7) If you have both PE-only and PC-managed clusters, confirm which endpoint the TA is hitting; a PC that proxies multiple clusters should still show distinct `cluster_name` values, but a mis-keyed `nutanix_cluster_owner` entry will blank `owner_email` for an otherwise valid alert.

(8) Cross-check the forbidden fiction: a search for `sourcetype="nutanix:cvm"` on an honest 5076 install must return no rows. If you see rows, a human renamed a HEC field—fix props, not this UC’s semantics.

(9) Time sync: if `_time` is hours behind `_indextime`, the `last_status_ev` column still reads old but the CVM is fine; fix NTP on the poller, not the threshold.

(10) Acropolis: on ESxi-only clusters, `NA` is a normal `service_status` for that service. If your estate is 100% AHV and you still only see `NA`, you likely pointed the TA at a partial endpoint—re-read the add-on’s supported-api matrix.

### **Step 4 — Operationalize**

Schedule the search every five minutes with the same `earliest=-2h@h` window as the interactive proof so transient start states clear without widening the lookback. Route `cvm_severity=critical` to the Nutanix platform on-call; route `high` to ServiceNow P2; route `medium` to a business-hours channel for Pithos and Hera classes unless your storage CAB elevates them. Throttle on `cluster_name`+`cvm_ip` for forty-five minutes, allow immediate re-page if the severity escalates from `high` to `critical` in the same incident because the boolean bits show a new `bad_stg` or `bad_gen` flip.

Dashboard layout: first row the alert table; second row a single value of CVMs in `critical` over the lookback; third row a list of CVMs currently in `in_maint` (optional child search) so the NOC can explain an empty first row during a change. Wire drilldown from `cvm_ip` to a prebuilt `nutanix:host` form search and from `block_serial` to the CMDB panel your hardware team already trusts. Document owners in the runbook: storage platform L3 owns Stargate and Cassandra interpretation, hypervisor L2 owns the host maintenance bit, the service catalog owner in `business_service` only receives FYI for `medium`.

Change freezes: the lookup carries `change_freeze_until` for audit context. Policy decision: never auto-resolve ServiceNow on freeze; add an annotation that the freeze was active so CAB can still see that someone acknowledged the event even when paging is suppressed for lower tiers. If you must page through freezes for Stargate `DOWN`, do it in a child search that only checks `bad_stg=1`, not by editing the canonical UC text without a version bump in git.

### **Step 5 — Tune & extend**

When AOS 7.x changes Genesis labels again, re-run `| fieldsummary` and extend `skey` membership before you add new product services (for example a renamed insights daemon). If Curator is too chatty, require two consecutive `DOWN` polls before `bad_cur` drives `high`—implement that as a `streamstats` window on a summary index, not as a one-off `search` in the main alert, so you can still reason about the base SPL. For stretched clusters, duplicate `cvm_ip` in rare DNS flip events may appear; join on `node_uuid` if your TA enriches it on both sides even when the IP changes, or extend the `join` keys with your CMDB’s stable node id when Splunk normalizes that field. When Nutanix ships a new minor TA that renames `serviceStatus` to another camelCase, add it to the `coalesce` list here and in the saved-search default—never fork two canonical SPLs.

If the estate grows so large that the `join` is slow, precompute a nightly `| outputlookup` of `(cluster_name,cvm_ip,host_name,node_position,block_serial)` and replace the `join` with `lookup`—fresher than midnight only if you add an hourly version for operations teams that accept five-minute staleness on serial numbers. If you add Zookeeper to the service list, confirm with your TAE whether 5076 even emits it on your API level; the historical template that referenced Zookeeper in old drafts is not a guarantee the field exists in your tenant.

Troubleshooting quick hits: zero rows in Step 1 means modular input or network to Prism, not Splunk’s query language. Rows in Search but an empty alert means `in_maint=1` for every CVM the search would normally return—read Prism maintenance, not the alert JSON. A sudden burst of `bad_acp=1` on an ESxi cluster after a field rename is almost always a mis-parse of `NA`—open `props.conf` before you open a bridge ticket.
grandmaExplanation: Each physical box has a tiny helper machine that does the heavy storage work for the virtual machines on that box. We watch those helpers one by one so we know when one is not doing its job, because the rest of the cluster has to pick up the slack and things get slow. We tell the right team which single helper needs help before most people would even feel the drag.
hardware: Nutanix NX NX-8000, NX-9000, and related NX families; Dell EMC XC and later Dell Integrated System for Nutanix nodes; Lenovo ThinkAgile HX and Lenovo DSS AHV offerings; HPE ProLiant, HPE DX, and Apollo-style Nutanix-ready sleds; Inspur InMerge for Nutanix; Crystal Group Rugged and other short-depth factory images where Acropolis and CVMs still run the same REST contracts; Cisco UCS C-Series, B-Series, and X-Series profiles sold as Nutanix on Cisco UCS when AOS 6.5 LTS, 6.6, 6.7, 6.8, or 7.0 is under support; plus Fujitsu PRIMEFLEX and any other ODM block that still presents Prism Element. AHV is the native hypervisor; the same CVM process stack is present on ESXi- or Hyper-V-hosted legacy clusters, where the CVM is still a guest with one instance per node and the data path semantics match even though the surrounding hypervisor is different. Document whether the block is all-flash or hybrid so on-call knows expected rebuild and Curator windows.
